column1: What is the Discovery tactic (TA0007)?
column2: An adversary attempts to enumerate information about the environment they're operating in — including systems, resources, users, misconfigurations, and vulnerabilities — typically as a prerequisite to lateral movement

column1: What privilege level is required for basic Active Directory enumeration queries?
column2: Standard domain user — queries for users, groups, OUs, GPOs, etc. can all be performed without elevated privileges

column1: What protocols are commonly used for Active Directory enumeration?
column2: LDAP (port 389), LDAPS (port 636), and ADWS (Active Directory Web Services), which wraps LDAP queries in HTTP requests

column1: What is BloodHound?
column2: A tool created by SpecterOps to help security professionals discover, understand, and visualise attack paths within identity providers such as on-prem Active Directory and Entra ID

column1: What are the two editions of BloodHound and their intended audiences?
column2: Community Edition is designed for offensive security use; Enterprise Edition is designed for defence

column1: What tools are used to collect identity data for BloodHound?
column2: SharpHound, AzureHound, and BloodHound.py

column1: Why is running default BloodHound collectors risky against a mature security team?
column2: Security vendors have signatured the hardcoded queries, network traffic for session enumeration, and large/slow AD data retrievals — LDAP queries can also be logged both client-side and server-side

column1: What is the recommended alternative approach to running default BloodHound collectors?
column2: Run custom data collection queries using a tool like ldapsearch, then use BOFHound to parse the output into BloodHound-compatible JSON files that can be imported into the UI

column1: What is LDAP and how does a client establish a session?
column2: An industry standard protocol for accessing directory service information — clients establish a session with a domain controller on TCP port 389 (LDAP) or 636 (LDAPS), authenticated via domain context or explicit credentials

column1: What is an objectClass in Active Directory LDAP?
column2: An attribute associated with every directory object that defines the types of attributes that object can have

column1: What are some common Active Directory objectClass values?
column2: computer, domain, group, person, groupPolicyContainer, organizationalUnit, and trustedDomain

column1: Why can searching for all 'user' or 'person' objects return unexpected results?
column2: Computer accounts have objectClass values of person, organizationalPerson, user, and computer — so querying for person or user will also return computer accounts

column1: Why should indexed fields be preferred in LDAP queries?
column2: Searching by indexed fields is significantly faster than non-indexed fields, and choosing efficient queries is also relevant to OPSEC

column1: What is the sAMAccountType value used to filter for regular user accounts?
column2: 805306368 (0x30000000 in hex), which corresponds to SAM_NORMAL_USER_ACCOUNT

column1: What does the adminCount attribute being set to 1 indicate on a user object?
column2: The user account is or was a member of a privileged group that is protected by the AdminSDHolder mechanism

column1: How are AND conditions represented in LDAP filter syntax?
column2: Using the & character at the beginning of the grouping — e.g. (&(condition1)(condition2))

column1: How are OR conditions represented in LDAP filter syntax?
column2: Using the
column3: character at the beginning of the grouping — e.g. (

column1: How are NOT/exclusion conditions represented in LDAP filter syntax?
column2: Using the ! character — e.g. (!(attribute=value))

column1: What is the LDAP_MATCHING_RULE_BIT_AND OID and what is it used for?
column2: OID 1.2.840.113556.1.4.803 — used to perform a bitwise AND operation, particularly useful for querying flag-based attributes like userAccountControl

column1: What is the userAccountControl attribute?
column2: A set of bitwise flags on user and computer objects that define various account properties and states, such as delegation configuration or whether the account is disabled

column1: What bitwise value is used to find computers configured for unconstrained delegation?
column2: 524288, which corresponds to the TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION flag in userAccountControl

column1: What is the LDAP_MATCHING_RULE_IN_CHAIN OID and what is it used for?
column2: OID 1.2.840.113556.1.4.1941 — used to query the full ancestry chain of an object, allowing recursive group membership resolution without manually querying each nested group

column1: What is the Expensive Search Results Threshold detection?
column2: A server-side detection that triggers when a query returns more results than a configured threshold — caused by extremely broad queries like (objectClass=*)

column1: What is the Search Time Threshold detection?
column2: A server-side detection that triggers when a query takes longer than a configured number of milliseconds — affected by result size and the number of attributes requested

column1: What attributes contribute most to slow LDAP query times?
column2: Requesting all attributes with wildcards combined with ntsecuritydescriptor produces the slowest queries — limiting attributes to essentials like samaccounttype, distinguishedname, objectsid, and ntsecuritydescriptor reduces this

column1: What is the Inefficient Search Results Threshold detection?
column2: A server-side detection that triggers when a query visits more than a configured number of objects but returns fewer than 10% of them — even targeted, narrow queries can trigger this if the domain is large

column1: What strategy helps avoid the Inefficient Search Results Threshold?
column2: Enumerating all relevant attributes for all objects of a type in a single query — returning all visited objects makes the query efficient, and keeping the attribute list small keeps it fast

column1: What is BOFHound?
column2: A Python script (not a BOF) that parses the raw output of ldapsearch and other tools into BloodHound-compatible JSON files that can be imported into the BloodHound UI

column1: What does an object in BloodHound represented only by a SID or 'no name or id' indicate?
column2: No data has been collected on that object yet — a targeted LDAP query using its objectsid should be run to gather its attributes

column1: What is restricted group data in the context of BloodHound?
column2: Local group memberships defined and applied via GPOs — this data cannot be collected via LDAP alone and must be read from policy files in SYSVOL, otherwise BloodHound will be missing important edges

column1: Where is restricted group membership data stored within a GPO?
column2: In a file called GptTmpl.inf inside the Machine's SecEdit folder within the GPO's SYSVOL path

column1: What format does the GptTmpl.inf Group Membership section use to express membership?
column2: It maps a domain group SID to a local group SID using the format: *<domain-group-SID>__Memberof = *<local-group-SID>

column1: What is the built-in Windows Administrators local group SID?
How can manually discovered admin relationships be added to BloodHound?
column2: By running a custom Cypher MERGE query to create an AdminTo edge between the relevant computer and group objects using their SIDs

column1: What are WMI filters in the context of GPOs?
column2: Additional criteria applied to a GPO that use WMI queries to conditionally restrict which computers the GPO is applied to — for example, limiting application to computers running a specific Windows version

column1: What is the relationship between WMI filters and GPOs?
column2: A one-to-many relationship — a GPO can only have one WMI filter applied at a time, but a single WMI filter can be applied to multiple GPOs

column1: How is a WMI filter linked to a GPO in Active Directory?
column2: By setting the GPO's gPCWQLFilter attribute to reference the GUID of the WMI filter object

column1: Where are WMI filter objects stored in Active Directory?
column2: In the CN=System,CN=WmiPolicy,CN=SOM container as objects of class msWMI-Som

column1: What attributes of an msWMI-Som object are important for understanding a WMI filter?
column2: msWMI-Name contains the display name and msWMI-Parm2 contains the actual WMI query

column1: Why is BloodHound's handling of WMI filters a limitation?
column2: At the time of writing, BloodHound does not collect, display, or evaluate WMI filters when building its graphs — meaning GPO-to-computer edges may appear to apply when they actually do not due to a filter

column1: What recommended query methodology minimises OPSEC risk during AD enumeration?
column2: Performing small, efficient, targeted LDAP queries spread over several days or weeks rather than large bulk collections — building up a picture of the environment gradually
